text: The DaVinci Endoscope Plus camera provides a magnified HD image of the surgical field.

The surgeon addresses an incarcerated ventral incisional hernia, with clear visualization of the tissue planes between the serosa of the bowel and the abdominal wall. The adhesions are not dense, allowing for easy identification of anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses a forced bipolar instrument in the left hand to gently grasp the bowel. The instrument is designed for both gentle and stronger grasping, suitable for handling mesh and suturing, and provides a safe energy source for operating on the bowel.

The surgical team arranges the ports on the patient's left abdominal wall, lateral to the midclavicular line. The abdominal wall is cleared of adhesions, preparing for the placement of additional ports to perform the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon addresses substantial adhesions between the liver and the abdominal wall, preparing to lyse these adhesions as a necessary step to proceed with the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses blunt movements and sharp dissection to release the small bowel adhesions from the liver edge, with the adhesions separating easily.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis near the bowel using blunt and sharp dissection, avoiding cautery. The Endoscope Plus provides a clear view of the dissection plane, facilitating safe and straightforward separation of the bowel from the liver capsule.
The surgeon begins to slowly dissect and expose the medial part of the gallbladder wall during a robotic-assisted multiport cholecystectomy, with visible robotic arms arranged for optimal access.

The assistant uses a laparoscopic retractor to elevate the gallbladder over the liver. With the left segment of the liver freed from adhesions, the gallbladder is positioned for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon measures the size of the defect, preparing the mesh for use during the cholecystectomy. The assistant places two additional ports across the midline to facilitate the procedure.
A port is placed through the hernia defect to facilitate the removal of the gallbladder with stones and the introduction of mesh into the abdomen.

The surgeon dissects one-third of the way up the gallbladder fossa before applying three clips on the cystic duct and three clips on the cystic artery. Both structures are then transected using electrocautery.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the fossa and places it into an endocatch bag. The bag is then advanced through the port placed through the abdominal wall defect, avoiding the need for dilation of the port site and abdominal wall during removal.
The surgeon advances the mesh through the hernia defect, utilizing the same pathway that was used for the gallbladder removal, avoiding the need for a larger twelve millimeter port or incision.
The surgeon closes the ventral hernia defects using a force bipolar instrument, which aids in needle manipulation and suturing by providing a stronger grasp.
The surgeon uses a twelve-inch absorbable V-lock suture to close the abdominal wall, avoiding permanent sutures.

The surgeon prepares to place an 18 by 22 centimeter Ovitex mesh, which is a biologic mesh with a permanent proline matrix, into the surgical field. Due to the clean contaminated nature of the case following cholecystectomy, a full synthetic mesh is avoided.

The assistant secures two separate zero-V-lock sutures at the far corners of the mesh. The surgeon fixes the mesh to the patient's right lower quadrant with a few suture throws to ensure it is centered, then repeats the process in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon lifts the mesh to ensure it is centered and adequately covers the defect. The top border of the mesh is kept taut as a new suture is used to fix the mesh to the patient's left upper quadrant. The surgeon continues the suture line down the left side of the abdomen, with the large piece of mesh visible in front of the camera.

The surgeon resumes suturing the lower border of the mesh in the patient's right lower quadrant, then moves to the right upper quadrant to continue suturing the upper border. The needle is directed out of the abdominal wall approximately one to two centimeters above the mesh border to maintain tension.

The surgeon begins a new suture line in the right lower quadrant to complete the right-sided border of the mesh. An additional diagonal suture is placed down the midline of the mesh, although a midline suture is typically more common.
